---
title: "Using spectator - worked examples"
# output: html_notebook
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
# output: pdf_document
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Using spectator - worked examples}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r label = "knitr options", include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
    fig.width = 7, 
    fig.height = 5,
    fig.align = "center",
    collapse = TRUE,
    comment = "#>"
)
knitr::opts_knit$set(global.device = TRUE)
```
## Introduction

The spectator package for R was developed to allow access to 
[Spectator Earth](https://spectator.earth/) API from R. Spectator Earth offers a Web app providing Earth Observation imagery, mainly from open data satellites like the Sentinel and the Landsat family. These features are also exposed through an API, and the goal of the spectator package is to provide easy access to this functionality from R.

The main functions allow to retrieve the acquisition plans for Sentinel-1, Sentinel-2 and Landsat-8 satellites and to get the past or (near)future overpasses over an area of interest for these satellites. It is also possible to search the archive for available images over the area of interest for a given (past) period, get the URL links to download the whole image tiles, or alternatively to download the image for just the area of interest based on selected spectral bands.

One can also get a current position and trajectory for a much larger set of satellites.

Other functions might be added in subsequent releases of the package.

## API key
Some of the functions (mainly those specific to Sentinel and Landsat satellites) require to pass an API key as a parameter to the function (because the underlying API endpoint requires it). The API key is automatically generated for every registered user at https://app.spectator.earth. You can find it under 'Your profile' (bottom left button) and copy it to clipboard. The functions in the spectator package by default retrieve the API key from the environment variable "spectator_earth_api_key". You can choose any other way of providing it, but keep in mind that for security reasons it is NOT recommended to hard-code (include it as clear text) it in your scripts.

```{r label = "setup", include = FALSE}
library(spectator)
```
## Satellites

get all satellites withe their positions
```{r label = "get satellites"}
pos <- GetAllSatellites(positions = TRUE)
head(pos)
```

do some nice graphs

```{r}
library(sf)
library(maps)
maps::map("world", fill = TRUE, col = "lightgrey")
```

show open data satellites in green
show others in red
```{r}
plot(sf::st_geometry(subset(pos, open == TRUE)), add = TRUE, col = "green", pch = 15)
plot(sf::st_geometry(subset(pos, open == FALSE)), add = TRUE, col = "red", pch = 16)
```


add labels
shift labels up to be able to read them
```{r}
xy <- sf::st_coordinates(pos)
xy[, 2] <- xy[, 2] + 3 
text(xy, labels = pos$name, cex = 0.5)

```
```{r, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_knit$set(global.device = FALSE)
```

## Trajectories

get trajectory and current position for a selected satellite


```{r}
sat <- "SPOT-7"
traj <- GetTrajectory(satellite = sat)
pos <- GetSatellite(satellite = sat, positions = TRUE)

```


 do some nice graphs
```{r}
map("world", fill = TRUE, col = "lightgrey")
plot(sf::st_geometry(traj), lwd = 2, col = "red", add = TRUE)
plot(sf::st_geometry(pos), pch = 15, col = "green", cex = 1.5, add = TRUE)
title(main = sprintf("current %s trajectory & position", sat))

```

## Acquisition plans

examples for acquisition plans
works only for Sentinel-2, Landsat-8 and Sentinel-1 satellites

get plans for all eligible satellites for today

```{r}
plans <- GetAcquisitionPlan()
head(plans)
```

explore the content of the data frame, you'll see that the available attributes vary with the satellite

focus on Sentinel 2
day after tomorrow
```{r}
sat <- c("Sentinel-2A", "Sentinel-2B")
day <- Sys.Date()  + 2
plan <- GetAcquisitionPlan(satellites = sat, date = day)
head(plan)
```


do some nice graphs
```{r label = "plot acquisition plans"}
library(maps)
maps::map("world", fill = TRUE, col = "lightgrey")
plot(sf::st_geometry(plan), border = "red", add = TRUE)
title(main = sprintf("%s acquisition plan for %s", paste(sat, collapse = "/"), day))
```

## Satellite overpasses

get the Luxembourg country shape as area of interest
```{r}
dsn <- system.file("extdata", "luxembourg.geojson", package = "spectator")
boundary <- sf::read_sf(dsn, as_tibble = FALSE)

```

first check that we have the api_key
need api key; so get it first, all set it in code
```{r}
my_key <- Sys.getenv("spectator_earth_api_key")
```

look for Sentinel-2 A and B, use shorthand notation, default time frame
```{r}
pass <- GetOverpasses(boundary, satellites = "S-2", acquisitions = TRUE, api_key = my_key)
```

do some nice graphs
```{r}
library(maps)
days <- range(as.Date(pass$date))
satellites <- sort(unique(pass$satellite))
maps::map(database = "world", region = c("Belgium", "Netherlands", "Germany", "Luxembourg", "France", "Switzerland"), 
          col = "lightgrey", fill = TRUE)
plot(sf::st_geometry(boundary), add = TRUE, col = "red", border = FALSE)
plot(sf::st_geometry(pass), add = TRUE)
title(main = sprintf("%s overpasses for period %s", paste(satellites, collapse = "/"), 
                     paste(days, collapse = ":")))

```

look for Sentinel-1 A and B, use shorthand notation, last week  
```{r}
pass <- GetOverpasses(boundary, satellites = "S-1", days_before = 7, days_after = 0, acquisitions = TRUE)
```


do some nice graphs
```{r}
days <- range(as.Date(pass$date))
satellites <- sort(unique(pass$satellite))
maps::map(database = "world", region = c("Belgium", "Netherlands", "Germany", "Luxembourg", "France", "Switzerland"), 
          col = "lightgrey", fill = TRUE)
plot(sf::st_geometry(boundary), add = TRUE, col = "red", border = FALSE)
plot(sf::st_geometry(pass), add = TRUE)
title(main = sprintf("%s overpasses for period %s", paste(satellites, collapse = "/"), 
                     paste(days, collapse = ":")))
```

## Searching for images

first check that we have the api_key
need api key; so get it first, all set it in code
```{r}
my_key <- Sys.getenv("spectator_earth_api_key")
```

get the New York City Central Park shape as area of interest
```{r}
dsn <- system.file("extdata", "centralpark.geojson", package = "spectator")
boundary <- sf::read_sf(dsn, as_tibble = FALSE)
```


search for May 2021 Sentinel 2 images 
```{r}
catalog <- SearchImages(aoi = boundary, satellites = "S2", 
                        date_from = "2021-05-01", date_to = "2021-05-30", 
                        footprint = FALSE, api_key = my_key)
head(catalog)
```


## Imagery files
get the id of the image with minimal cloud coverage

```{r}
best_id <- catalog[order(catalog$cloud_cover_percentage), ][1, "id"]
```

list all downloadable files for the image with minimal cloud coverage
```{r}
images <- GetImageryFilesList(best_id, api_key = my_key)
head(images)
```

download the whole band 4 image tile for the image with minimal cloud coverage
```{r include=FALSE}
size <- as.integer(subset(images, path == "B04.jp2", "size"))
```

if you want to download this big file (`r prettyNum(size, big.mark = " ")` bytes) use the code below
```{r eval = FALSE}
from <- paste0(catalog[order(catalog$cloud_cover_percentage), ][1, "download_url"], "B04.jp2")
to <- "B04.jp2"
library(httr)
resp <- httr::GET(url = from, query = list(api_key = my_key))
writeBin(httr::content(resp), con = to)
```

## High resolution images

get the high resolution image of the Central Park
sentinel 2 bands (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sentinel-2#Spectral_bands).


```{r}
img <- GetHighResolutionImage(aoi = boundary, id = best_id, bands = c(4, 3, 2), 
                              width = 1024, height = 1024,
                              file = tempfile(pattern = "img", fileext = ".jpg"), 
                              api_key = my_key)
```
text above image  
![something meaningful](`r img`){width=95%}  
text below image
<br>
<br>
<br>
_Compiled on `r Sys.time()`._
